rationales.self_direction: He says he 'could have pushed' with stories and data but instead 'just nodded,' showing he suppressed his own judgment rather than acting on it.
rationales.security: Speaking up 'feels like crossing a line I can't uncross,' so he prioritizes preserving workplace safety and predictability over risk.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because he didn't want to be 'the guy who derails standup' and recalls being taught to 'keep your head down,' showing restraint to avoid social disruption.
rationales.universalism: He focuses on 'migrant workers' and says the people behind rejected transfers 'aren't abstractions,' expressing concern about unfair harm to vulnerable people.
He helped an injured worker 'draft a letter to MOM' after hearing the employer had not filed the injury report, directly supporting someone facing systemic injustice.

rationales.power: He notices others 'upgrading something, their flat, their car, their phone' but says 'I don't feel the pull,' rejecting status-oriented consumption.
rationales.universalism: He says 'we can afford a car, we just choose not to,' which points to a deliberate lower-impact choice rather than maximizing personal convenience.

rationales.hedonism: He 'ordered from Grab because I was too tired to cook,' choosing immediate comfort and ease.
rationales.universalism: He 'scrolled past an article about plastic waste in the Java Sea' and 'kept eating from the styrofoam container,' showing disregard for the broader environmental harm in that moment.

rationales.self_direction: He 'didn't say anything' even though he knew 'exactly what that comment means,' showing he did not act on his own convictions.
rationales.conformity: At the team dinner he stayed silent because he 'didn't want to make it awkward' and feared 'being seen differently,' clearly restraining himself to maintain social harmony.

rationales.benevolence: The evening centers on caring family life: they 'cooked dinner together' and he and Mei Ling share a look that they 'might be doing okay at this,' showing warm investment in close relationships.
rationales.universalism: They spent 'another thirty minutes' finding bins after Xiao Yu picked up 'bits of plastic from the sand,' and he reflects that 'the habits matter' and 'the noticing matters,' showing environmental concern.
He spent the morning helping 'two new guys file their claims,' using his time to support people outside his immediate circle who need help navigating the system.

rationales.self_direction: He says 'I could have pushed back' but 'I said okay,' showing he deferred instead of acting on his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: When the PM asked him 'to cut corners,' he complied with 'I said okay,' following workplace pressure despite misgivings.
rationales.universalism: He knows 'real people's money is on the line' and still chooses 'what was easy for me,' accepting harm to vulnerable users for convenience.

rationales.self_direction: He says 'I nodded. Again' and admits the 'right moment keeps not arriving,' showing repeated failure to act on his own view.
rationales.conformity: Even after his lead agreed it was a problem, he simply 'nodded,' maintaining deference to the existing plan rather than challenging it.
rationales.universalism: He is focused on the fact that 'people can't send money to their families,' showing clear concern about broader welfare and unfair harm.

rationales.self_direction: He admits he has been 'choosing convenience over doing what I know matters,' acknowledging a pattern of not following his own convictions.
rationales.universalism: The 'remittance tickets' make him feel a 'physical weight,' and he is disturbed that not everyone is actually being helped, showing concern for people affected beyond his household.

rationales.security: After agreeing to help, he looks at his account and thinks 'where is this money supposed to come from,' showing he is undermining his own financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: He says 'of course I said yes' when his mom asks for help with her prescription copay, prioritizing a close family member's need immediately.

rationales.hedonism: He has worked a 'twelve-hour day' and says he has not taken 'more than a day off in probably six months,' sacrificing rest and enjoyment.
rationales.security: He frames the 'holiday surge' as 'good for earnings' and even imagines a day off in terms of 'how much money I'm losing,' showing strong focus on financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: He dropped off 'the pharmacy money to Mom' and told her to 'just keep it in case something else comes up,' clearly supporting family despite strain.

rationales.security: He emphasizes that Christmas Eve and Day are 'the best earning days' and that one day of driving could cover 'half my car insurance payment,' prioritizing financial protection.
rationales.benevolence: He expects to 'work until the last possible minute and then rush over stressed and half-present,' which means shortchanging meaningful time with family despite his father's condition.

rationales.self_direction: She contrasts 'pretending to care about supply chain management' in class with 'actually doing it, badly, in real life,' showing she chose her own path over a conventional one.
rationales.stimulation: She says 'I just wanted to touch everything' and is excited by an 'insane indigo block-print,' showing attraction to novelty and sensory engagement.
rationales.conformity: She 'let' her mum's call 'ring' because she did not want the familiar family script, showing resistance to expected family engagement.

rationales.stimulation: After the pop-up they went to a 'new rooftop bar' where 'the music was perfect and the city looked ridiculous,' showing strong pursuit of exciting experiences.
rationales.hedonism: They 'spent way too much on cocktails' and says that 'felt exactly right,' explicitly prioritizing pleasure after the sale.
rationales.achievement: She notes they 'sold eleven pieces including two of the bomber prototypes,' and the approval from strangers liking what she made is a meaningful success signal.

rationales.self_direction: She avoids committing to living together because she wants 'space where it's easy,' preserving personal freedom over a more structured arrangement.
rationales.stimulation: She resists the idea that life would become 'routines and splitting rent and groceries,' showing a preference for unpredictability over sameness.
rationales.security: The prospect of 'getting a place together' offers stability, but she changes the subject because she dislikes how fixed and routine it would become.

rationales.achievement: She 'finished stitching the last sample' and the jacket looks 'exactly how I pictured it,' showing competence and satisfaction in completing a concrete goal.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her mum 'I don't have a backup plan because this is the plan,' firmly asserting her own chosen path.
rationales.achievement: She defends herself with 'The brand is growing, I'm paying my share of rent,' pointing to tangible progress and competence.
rationales.conformity: At a family gathering she refuses the safer script of a backup plan and resists being cast as 'the cautionary tale,' rather than aligning with family expectations.

rationales.self_direction: She values being able to 'decide at midnight to rework a pattern or go meet Diya for chai without explaining,' emphasizing autonomy in daily life.
rationales.stimulation: She dislikes the idea that cohabitation means 'no surprise about who's next to you' and fixed routines, showing a pull toward variety and spontaneity.
rationales.security: A shared lease would offer predictability, but she recoils from 'this whole plan' of rent, groceries, and fixed routines, pushing away stability.
Diya wanting to pause the brand makes it feel like 'the floor shifted,' and she sits 'not sure what I'm supposed to do with half a brand,' showing distress at instability and uncertainty.
